event: Paleoindian and early hunter-gatherer groups inhabit the region, leaving the oldest known traces of human presence in the Guiana coastal and interior zones.
Coastal Amerindian communities develop fishing, shellfish gathering, and early horticulture, forming shell middens along the Atlantic shore.
Amerindian peoples build extensive raised-field agricultural systems in the seasonally flooded savannas to grow crops above the waterline.
Pottery-making cultures linked to wider Amazonian and Caribbean traditions flourish, with Arawak- and Carib-speaking peoples established across the territory.
Indigenous nations including the Kalina (Galibi), Palikur, Wayana, Wayampi, and Lokono inhabit the coast and forested interior on the eve of European contact.
Christopher Columbus sights the Guiana coast during his third voyage, marking the first European observation of the region.
Spanish navigator Vicente Yáñez Pinzón explores parts of the Guiana coastline shortly after Columbus's passage.
An early French expedition led by Daniel de la Touche, sieur de la Ravardière, attempts to establish a presence on the Guiana coast.
Charles Poncet de Brétigny founds Cayenne, beginning sustained French colonization of the territory.
France's Compagnie des Indes occidentales takes control, organizing the colony around plantation agriculture and the Atlantic slave trade.
English forces briefly seize Cayenne before the colony is restored to France.
Dutch forces capture Cayenne, but France recovers the colony the following year and reaffirms its hold.
The disastrous Kourou expedition sends thousands of European settlers who die of disease, devastating France's colonization scheme.
The French Revolutionary government abolishes slavery in the colony following the decree of the National Convention.
Cayenne becomes a place of political deportation, exiling opponents of the French Revolution and later regimes (the 'dry guillotine').
Napoleon reestablishes slavery in French colonies, reversing the Revolutionary abolition in Guiana.
A combined Anglo-Portuguese force captures Cayenne, placing the colony under Portuguese (Brazilian) administration during the Napoleonic Wars.
France regains control of French Guiana under the terms following the Napoleonic Wars.
Slavery is permanently abolished in French Guiana, freeing the enslaved population and reshaping colonial society.
France establishes the notorious penal colony system, beginning the transportation of convicts to bagnes across the territory.
Gold is discovered in the interior, sparking a rush that draws migrants and reshapes the colonial economy.
Alfred Dreyfus is imprisoned on Devil's Island, making the offshore penal islands internationally infamous.
An arbitration award settles the long-running boundary dispute with Brazil over the contested Amapá territory in Brazil's favor.
France carves out the Inini interior territory as a separate administrative unit covering the vast forested hinterland.
France ends the sentencing of new convicts to the penal colony, beginning the long wind-down of the bagne system.
French Guiana becomes an overseas department (département d'outre-mer) of France, formally ending colonial status.
The penal colony is fully closed and the last convicts are repatriated, ending a century of transportation.
France selects Kourou as the site for its space center, transforming the region's economy and infrastructure.
The Guiana Space Centre at Kourou launches its first rocket, beginning operations as Europe's spaceport.
France's Diamant rocket is launched from Kourou, marking early sovereign space activity from the territory.
Inland populations and tribal communities receive expanded recognition as French Guiana modernizes its administration.
The European Ariane rocket programme launches its first flight from Kourou, establishing the spaceport's commercial role.
France's decentralization reforms create a regional council, giving French Guiana an elected regional assembly alongside its departmental status.
Thousands of Maroon and other refugees flee the Suriname civil war across the Maroni River into French Guiana.
Student and social protests over education and living conditions erupt in Cayenne, highlighting tensions with the metropole.
A French constitutional reform formally recognizes French Guiana's status as an overseas region and department.
Operation Harpie is launched to combat illegal gold mining (orpaillage) devastating the interior forests and rivers.
Voters reject greater autonomy in a referendum, choosing to remain a department while approving a later merger of the departmental and regional councils.
A Russian Soyuz rocket is launched from Kourou for the first time, expanding the spaceport's international partnerships.
The combined single territorial collectivity is approved, streamlining French Guiana's governance into one assembly.
A massive general strike and social movement paralyzes the territory, demanding investment in security, health, and infrastructure and yielding a multibillion-euro accord with Paris.
French Guiana, as part of France and the EU, continues hosting the Ariane and Vega launch programmes amid debate over its political future.
The inaugural flight of the Ariane 6 rocket lifts off from the Guiana Space Centre, securing Europe's independent access to space.
